original_abstract:
Over the past several decades, Bayesian Networks (BNs) have emerged as an indispensable framework for reasoning under uncertainty across a wide array of disciplines, including medicine, epidemiology, economics, and the social sciences. Their utility is particularly evident in real-world scenarios that require evaluating hypothetical evidence to guide complex intervention strategies. Despite their popularity, defining the underlying graphical structure of a BN—especially when intended for causal inference—remains a formidable challenge. Current solutions typically involve automated discovery from data, manual construction guided by expert knowledge, or hybrid methodologies that integrate both.

This paper presents a comprehensive review of combinatorial algorithms designed for learning BN structures from data. We examine 74 distinct algorithms, ranging from foundational, well-established techniques to modern, state-of-the-art approaches. By describing each algorithm using a consistent framework, we highlight their fundamental similarities and critical differences. Furthermore, we evaluate existing benchmarks and performance claims within the literature, addressing the often-inconsistent results reported across studies. Finally, we explore practical strategies for managing noisy real-world data and the ongoing efforts to incorporate domain expertise into the automated learning process.
humanized_abstract:
Bayesian Networks (BNs) have become a popular framework for reasoning under uncertainty in a large number of fields such as medicine, epidemiology, economics and social sciences. The BNs are particularly useful for modeling scenarios which involve the analysis of hypothetical evidence in order to decide the best strategy for interventions. However, the process of designing the structure of a BN – especially when it is focused on causal inference – is still considered a very challenging task. Most of the approaches currently available in literature try to address this challenge by carrying out one of the following tasks: a) algorithmic search based on data b) manual design from domain knowledge c) combinations of the two above approaches.

Learning Bayesian Networks from Data: A Survey of Combinatorial Algorithms We provide a detailed survey of work on combinatorial algorithms that learn the structure of Bayesian Networks (BNs) from data. The survey covers 74 different approaches, ranging from some of the oldest and most well studied to some of the newest advancements in the field. We present each algorithm in a standardized format to allow us to outline their common features and differences. We also critique current benchmarks and performance claims reported in literature due to widespread inconsistencies. Additionally, we highlight challenges that arise when learning from noised data commonly found in real-world datasets, as well as incorporating prior knowledge that is often available in such scenarios.
